summary: Gruber examines the practical differences between Apple's two new black iPhone 7 finishes — jet black and regular black — drawing on his hands-on experience at the launch event. He notes that jet black will inevitably show micro-abrasions and fingerprints over time, similar to the old iPhone 3G/3GS plastic backs. The regular black looks darker and better than space gray in person, though Apple's product photos don't capture this well. He concludes that the regular black looks better in everyday use while jet black feels better in hand, calling it the least slippery iPhone ever made.
key_points: Jet black will show micro-abrasions and fingerprints with use, and Apple themselves suggest using a case — making the color choice moot for case users since both blacks look nearly identical when encased
The jet black finish is grippy and tactile, making it the least slippery iPhone Apple has ever made, unlike the matte finish which feels identical across all color options
Apple's product photography undersells the regular black iPhone 7, which looks truly black in person rather than the dark gray it appears in photos
Gruber draws a practical distinction: regular black looks better in daily use, jet black feels better in hand but only looks stunning in pristine display conditions
money_quotes: If you're going to put your iPhone in a case, then it doesn't really matter whether you get regular black or jet black, because when encased, they're nearly indistinguishable.
Based on my (admittedly limited) time in the hands-on area, I'd call it the least slippery iPhone Apple has ever made.

I much preferred the anodized black iPhone 5 over the subsequent space gray models, even though the black iPhone 5 showed wear and tear easily. I thought it added character — call it a Millennium Falcon look.
My gut feeling from my time in the hands-on area is that in the real world, the black iPhone 7 looks better, and the jet black iPhone 7 feels better.
In the perfect vacuum of a retail display or photo studio, the jet black iPhone looks like the most stunning device I've ever seen. There's no way it's going to look like that in the real world.
The white iPhone people have had to make hard decisions on color for years. We black iPhone people have had it easy. Not this year, though.
key_insight: The jet black iPhone 7 is a tension between visual perfection and practical reality — stunning under ideal conditions but destined to show wear, while the regular black quietly looks better in everyday life.
